questions.stem: A 75-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of dull, deep pain in his right thigh. He has noticed a subtle bowing of the femur. He has no history of trauma. Blood tests show: ALP 450 U/L (normal 30-130), calcium 2.35 mmol/L, phosphate 1.0 mmol/L, PTH 4.5 pmol/L (normal 1.6-6.9). An X-ray of the femur shows cortical thickening, coarsened trabeculae, and a few fissure fractures. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of unilateral bone pain with bowing deformity, isolated raised ALP, normal calcium and PTH, and X-ray features of cortical thickening and coarsened trabeculae is classic for Paget disease. Osteomalacia typically presents with low calcium/phosphate and raised ALP; hyperparathyroidism shows hypercalcemia with raised PTH; metastatic prostate cancer is often sclerotic but multiple lesions and PSA elevation help differentiate; fibrous dysplasia usually presents younger with 'ground-glass' matrix on X-ray.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old asymptomatic woman is found to have an elevated ALP of 380 U/L (normal 30-130) on routine bloods. Liver function tests are otherwise normal. Calcium, phosphate, and PTH are normal. A bone scan reveals increased tracer uptake in the left hemipelvis and proximal femur. Her vitamin D level is adequate. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The isolated elevation of ALP with normal calcium, phosphate, and PTH in an asymptomatic patient points to Paget disease, especially with a bone scan showing focal uptake in typical sites. Osteomalacia usually has low vitamin D and abnormal calcium/phosphate; hyperparathyroidism has hypercalcemia and raised PTH; metastatic prostate cancer usually has a history of prostate cancer or elevated PSA; multiple myeloma typically presents with normal or mildly elevated ALP but with lytic lesions and often Bence-Jones protein.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with no known medical history trips and falls, sustaining a transverse fracture of his right femur. X-ray shows a displaced fracture through thickened bone with coarsened trabeculae and a widened cortex. His ALP is 520 U/L. What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The X-ray features of thickened bone with coarsened trabeculae and a transverse fracture are characteristic of Paget disease. Osteoporosis typically shows thin cortices without thickening; osteomalacia often presents with Looser zones; metastatic bone disease usually shows multiple lesions or a history of cancer; osteogenesis imperfecta presents in younger patients with blue sclera and family history.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known Paget disease of the lumbar spine now presents with progressive lower limb weakness, sensory loss in the legs, and urinary retention. On examination, he has a sensory level at T10. Spinal X-rays show an enlarged vertebral body at T10 with thickened cortices and coarse trabeculation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Paget disease of bone with spinal cord compression
questions.explanation: The known history of Paget disease and the X-ray appearance of an enlarged vertebral body with thickened cortices point to Paget disease as the cause of spinal stenosis. Metastatic compression usually has lytic/sclerotic lesions without vertebral body enlargement; disc prolapse is often at a lower level and without X-ray changes; tuberculous spondylitis involves discitis and paravertebral abscess; osteoporotic fracture shows vertebral wedging but not thickening.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss over 2 years. She has no tinnitus or vertigo. Otoscopy is normal. She also mentions mild left hip pain on walking. Blood tests show ALP 290 U/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hearing loss in Paget disease is often due to skull involvement causing compression of the cochlear nerve or ossicular disturbances. The combination of sensorineural hearing loss with elevated ALP and hip pain makes Paget disease the most likely. Otosclerosis typically presents with conductive hearing loss and normal ALP; presbycusis is age-related without bone pain; acoustic neuroma usually causes unilateral symptoms with tinnitus; Ménière disease includes vertigo and is episodic.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a three-month history of dull, deep pain in his left tibia. X-ray of the tibia shows a mixed lytic-sclerotic lesion with cortical thickening and bone enlargement. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of Paget disease of bone?
questions.explanation: The X-ray shows classic features of Paget disease, and a raised serum alkaline phosphatase (ALP) of bone origin would confirm active disease. ALP is the first-line biochemical test; a normal ALP does not exclude it, but an elevated level supports the diagnosis. Biopsy is rarely needed, and bone scan is for extent, not confirmation.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman has an incidental finding of isolated raised serum alkaline phosphatase (2.5× upper limit of normal) on routine bloods. She is asymptomatic. Her liver function tests are otherwise normal. What is the single most appropriate next investigation to establish the diagnosis of Paget disease?
options: Repeat liver function tests with gamma-glutamyl transferase
questions.explanation: Isolated raised ALP of bone origin in an asymptomatic patient is suspicious for Paget disease. The first-line imaging is a plain X-ray of the skull and pelvis, as these are common sites. If X-ray shows typical features, the diagnosis is confirmed. Bone scan is second-line for extent, not initial diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with known Paget disease of the left hemipelvis sustains a low-energy fracture of the left femoral neck. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm that the fracture has occurred through pagetic bone?
questions.explanation: Plain X-ray will show typical pagetic changes (coarse trabeculae, cortical thickening, bone expansion) in the proximal femur and pelvis, confirming the fracture is in abnormal bone. MRI and CT are not needed for this purpose; bone scan is less specific and ALP does not localize the site.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known polyostotic Paget disease has a serum alkaline phosphatase level 4 times the upper limit of normal. What is the most likely interpretation of this result?
options: Development of osteosarcoma in pagetic bone
questions.explanation: Serum total ALP is a marker of bone turnover in Paget disease; a fourfold elevation indicates active disease. Malignant transformation (osteosarcoma) is rare and would present with new severe pain, swelling, or a lytic lesion on X-ray, not just a rise in ALP. Liver enzymes were normal in the vignette.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with unilateral thigh pain has a normal serum alkaline phosphatase. X-ray of the femur shows a well-defined lucent lesion with cortical thickening but no periosteal reaction. What is the single best next test to establish the diagnosis of Paget disease?
questions.explanation: Up to 15% of patients with Paget disease, especially monostotic, have a normal total ALP. Bone-specific ALP (or P1NP) is more sensitive and can confirm bone turnover. However, X-ray features are diagnostic; in this case, the X-ray is already suggestive. The question asks for the best test to establish diagnosis; a bone turnover marker like bone-specific ALP can provide biochemical confirmation. Bone scan is for extent, not diagnosis when X-ray is doubtful.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is incidentally found to have an elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP) of 450 IU/L (normal <120). Examination is normal. X-ray shows mixed lytic-sclerotic changes in the right hemipelvis and skull thickening consistent with Paget disease of bone. He has no pain or other symptoms. What is the most appropriate management?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends bisphosphonate therapy for asymptomatic Paget disease if there is evidence of active disease (e.g., elevated ALP) at sites that may lead to future complications (e.g., skull involvement). Oral alendronic acid is first-line due to lower cost and good efficacy. Intravenous zoledronic acid is reserved for severe disease or intolerance to oral therapy.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known Paget disease presents with discomfort in her left tibia. ALP is 250 IU/L (normal <120). She has not received any treatment before. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
questions.explanation: For mild symptoms of Paget disease, first-line therapy is an oral bisphosphonate such as alendronic acid. This treats the underlying bone turnover and reduces pain. Paracetamol or NSAIDs only provide symptomatic relief without modifying disease progression.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with known Paget disease presents with acute onset of bilateral lower limb weakness and urinary retention over 24 hours. MRI shows spinal stenosis at T12-L1 due to Pagetic bone hypertrophy. He has an ALP of 800 IU/L. What is the most appropriate immediate therapy?
questions.explanation: Acute spinal cord compression from Paget disease is a neurosurgical emergency. Urgent decompression is required to prevent permanent neurological damage. Bisphosphonates are adjunctive to reduce disease activity but are not the primary treatment for acute cord compression.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with known Paget disease involving the skull has noticed progressive hearing loss over the past year. He has mild pain in his right hip. ALP is 350 IU/L. What is the most appropriate therapy to delay further hearing loss?
options: Surgical decompression of the inner ear
questions.explanation: Hearing loss in Paget disease is due to bony involvement of the cochlea. Treating the underlying condition with a bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronic acid) can slow progression. Hearing aids or implants address established loss but do not prevent deterioration.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with Paget disease and chronic kidney disease stage 4 (eGFR 25 mL/min/1.73m²) presents with bone pain. She has not been on bisphosphonates before. Which of the following treatments is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Intravenous zoledronic acid is contraindicated if creatinine clearance is <35 mL/min due to risk of renal toxicity. Alendronic acid and risedronate can be used with caution. Calcitonin is safe in renal impairment, though not first-line.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of dull pain in his left thigh. There is no swelling or tenderness. He has no past medical history. Blood tests show: ALP 450 U/L (40–130), calcium 2.35 mmol/L, phosphate 1.1 mmol/L, eGFR >60. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order a plain radiograph of the left femur
Refer for physiotherapy and simple analgesia
questions.explanation: Plain radiography is the first imaging step to confirm Paget's disease by showing characteristic features (osteolysis, bone expansion, cortical thickening). Treatment with bisphosphonates should only be initiated after definitive diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known Paget's disease of the right hemipelvis (confirmed on X-ray 2 years ago) complains of mild right groin pain (2/10 visual analogue scale). ALP is 180 U/L (normal <120). She is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Monitor with repeat ALP in 6 months
Refer to an orthopaedic surgeon for joint replacement
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend bisphosphonate therapy for symptomatic Paget's disease, even if symptoms are mild, to reduce pain and prevent complications. Mild pain does not justify watchful waiting when ALP is raised.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with known Paget's disease of the left tibia (on bisphosphonate therapy 2 years ago, now off treatment) presents with acute-onset severe pain and swelling over the mid-tibia after tripping on the kerb. He cannot weight-bear. X-ray shows a transverse lucent line in the anterior mid-shaft with some periosteal reaction. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immobilise in a below-knee cast and advise non-weight-bearing
Check serum calcium and refer for bone mineral density scan
Arrange urgent orthopaedic review for surgical fixation
questions.explanation: The X-ray shows a pseudofracture (Looser zone) – an incomplete fracture typical of Paget's. Immediate immobilisation and non-weight-bearing is required to prevent progression to a complete fracture. Bisphosphonate therapy can be considered later to treat the underlying disease.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known Paget's disease (involving the skull, diagnosed 5 years ago but not on treatment) now has progressive right-sided hearing loss over 6 months. He has no vertigo or tinnitus. Otoscopy is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to an ENT surgeon for stapedectomy
Arrange a CT scan of the temporal bones
questions.explanation: In Paget's disease, hearing loss is often due to cochlear involvement or ossicular chain fixation by pagetic bone. Bisphosphonate therapy can reduce disease activity and may slow or stabilise hearing loss; it is the first-line systemic treatment.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with Paget's disease of the right femur completed a 2-month course of oral risedronate 30 mg daily 6 months ago. She is now asymptomatic and her ALP has normalised. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange a repeat plain radiograph of the femur
Start calcium and vitamin D supplements
Refer for DXA scan of hip and spine
questions.explanation: After successful treatment (symptom resolution and ALP normalisation), bisphosphonate should be stopped and the patient monitored with periodic ALP measurements to detect relapse. Maintenance therapy is not routine.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known polyostotic Paget disease of bone presents with a 6-week history of worsening pain in his left tibia. He has noticed a new swelling over the anterior shin. On examination, the swelling is tender and warm. Which characteristic of the swelling most raises concern for malignant transformation to Paget's sarcoma?
questions.explanation: A palpable, non-bony mass indicates a soft tissue component beyond the bone, which is a hallmark of sarcomatous change. Tenderness and warmth are common in active Paget disease and are not specific. Skin pigmentation and lymphadenopathy are not typical features of Paget's sarcoma.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with extensive Paget disease (skull, pelvis, both femurs) presents with progressive dyspnoea on exertion and peripheral oedema. Her heart rate is 100 bpm, blood pressure 125/55 mmHg. Which clinical sign is most specific for high-output heart failure due to Paget disease?
questions.explanation: Wide pulse pressure (e.g., >60 mmHg) is characteristic of high-output cardiac failure, reflecting decreased systemic vascular resistance from vascular shunting in pagetic bone. Displaced apex beat, raised JVP, and third heart sound can occur in any type of heart failure, but wide pulse pressure is a specific sign of high output.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with Paget disease of the lumbar spine complains of low back pain radiating to both legs, leg weakness, and difficulty walking. Which examination finding is a red flag for cauda equina syndrome?
questions.explanation: Perianal sensory loss (saddle anaesthesia) indicates involvement of sacral nerve roots and is a classic red flag for cauda equina syndrome, requiring urgent MRI. Reduced ankle reflexes and extensor plantars are not specific to cauda equina; straight leg raise and local tenderness suggest nerve root irritation or mechanical pain.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old housebound woman with Paget disease is admitted after a fall. She is confused, dehydrated, and has polyuria. Which laboratory result is most likely to require immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: A calcium level of 3.1 mmol/L indicates severe hypercalcemia, a life-threatening emergency in Paget disease due to immobilization-induced bone resorption. Alkaline phosphatase elevation is typical of active Paget disease but is not an acute threat. Hypophosphatemia, PTH in range, and mild AKI are less urgent.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with Paget disease of the skull presents with progressive headache, dizziness, and hoarseness of voice. Which sign on examination suggests brainstem compression from basilar invagination?
questions.explanation: A poor gag reflex indicates involvement of the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves (CN IX and X), which are often compressed at the skull base in basilar invagination. Nystagmus may occur but is less specific; papilloedema implies raised intracranial pressure; hearing loss is a common pagetic symptom but not a red flag for emergent compression; trigeminal neuralgia is not typically due to Paget disease.
